content.text:
{When I look at your heavens, the work of your fingers, the moon and the stars, which you have set in place, what is man that you are mindful of him?}{Psalm 8:3–4}
 {Geometry is the archetype of the beauty of the world.}{— Johannes Kepler}

The determinant has a beautiful geometric meaning: it measures how a 
linear transformation changes area (in 2D) or volume (in 3D).

$ 
as a transformation that maps the unit square to a parallelogram.

content.title: Determinant as Area Scale Factor
content.content:
If $A$ is a $2 \times 2$ matrix and $S$ is any region in $\R^2$, then 
the area of $A(S)$ (the image of $S$ under the transformation) is:
\[
\text{Area}(A(S)) = |\det(A)| \cdot \text{Area}(S)
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Area calculation
content.problem:
The matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$ has 
$\det(A) = 12 - 2 = 10$. Thus $A$ multiplies all areas by a factor of 
$10$. A unit circle of area $\pi$ is transformed into an ellipse of 
area $10\pi$.

content.text: The **sign** of the determinant indicates orientation:

items:
$\det(A) > 0$: The transformation preserves orientation 
 (counterclockwise remains counterclockwise).

$\det(A) < 0$: The transformation reverses orientation 
 (a reflection is involved).

$\det(A) = 0$: The transformation collapses space to a 
 lower dimension.

content.title: Orientation
content.problem:
The rotation matrix 
$R_{90°} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ has 
$\det(R) = 1$, preserving orientation and area.

The reflection $F = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$ 
has $\det(F) = -1$, reversing orientation but preserving area.

content.text: In three dimensions, the determinant measures volume:

content.title: Determinant as Volume Scale Factor
content.content:
If $A$ is a $3 \times 3$ matrix with columns $\vect{a}_1$, 
$\vect{a}_2$, $\vect{a}_3$, then $|\det(A)|$ equals the volume of 
the parallelepiped (3D parallelogram) spanned by these vectors.

For any region $V$ in $\R^3$:
\[
\text{Volume}(A(V)) = |\det(A)| \cdot \text{Volume}(V)
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Volume of a parallelepiped
content.problem:
Find the volume of the parallelepiped with edges 
$\vect{a} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, 
$\vect{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$, 
$\vect{c} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$.

content.text:
In $\R^3$, the **cross product** of two vectors can be computed 
using a determinant:
\[

This gives a vector perpendicular to both $\vect{u}$ and $\vect{v}$, 
with magnitude equal to the area of the parallelogram they span.

Who has measured the waters in the 
hollow of his hand and marked off the heavens with a span?

marginNotes.author: Josiah Willard Gibbs (1839--1903)
marginNotes.text:
The whole is simpler 
than the sum of its parts.
